g.data-narration: This diagram explains the effects of e-waste pollutants on human health and environment for engineering students.
g.text: EFFECTS OF E-WASTE POLLUTANTS ON HUMAN HEALTH & ENVIRONMENT

g.data-narration: E-waste includes discarded electrical devices like computers, phones, and appliances at end-of-life stage.
g.text: 83% ends up in landfills or exported

g.data-narration: The major e-waste pollutants include heavy metals, persistent organic pollutants, flame retardants, and PVC.
The big four heavy metals in e-waste are lead, mercury, cadmium, and chromium, each causing severe health issues.
g.text: Persists for decades, bioaccumulates in living organisms

g.data-narration: POPs like dioxins, PCBs, PBDEs, and phthalates are toxic organic compounds that persist in the environment.
Humans are exposed through inhalation of burning fumes, ingestion of contaminated food and water, and dermal contact.
g.text: Workers in informal recycling face HIGHEST risk

g.data-narration: Lead causes cognitive deficits while mercury leads to sensory disturbance. Chromium and dioxins are carcinogenic.
PBDEs and phthalates disrupt endocrine function causing fertility issues, while cadmium damages kidneys.
Heavy metals contaminate soil for centuries while leachate pollutes groundwater and aquatic environments.
g.text: Heavy metals persist for centuries. Reduces microbial
activity. Contaminates food crops. Creates dead zones
Bioaccumulation in fish. Affects drinking water
Key point: Once contaminated, environments take decades to recover

g.data-narration: Open burning releases dioxins and particulate matter while biodiversity suffers from soil and aquatic contamination.
g.text: Open burning yields dioxins, furans, particulate matter
Low-temp burning below 850C yields more dioxins
Soil organisms affected. Disrupts nutrient cycling
Food chain transfer affects mammals and birds

g.data-narration: In India, informal recycling uses open burning, acid leaching, and landfilling causing severe community exposure.
g.text: Primitive techniques maximize toxin exposure and environmental release:
Often in residential areas. Entire communities exposed. Delhi, Moradabad examples

g.data-narration: Toxins biomagnify through the food chain from plants to humans, with concentrations increasing at each level.
CRT monitors release 300kg of lead while burning plastic releases dioxins that enter the food chain causing cancer.
g.text: Dioxins settle on fields. Food chain. Cancer risk
Solution: Use authorized recyclers. Controlled incineration above 850C. Engineered landfill liners

g.data-narration: India E-Waste Rules 2022 implement EPR with CPCB oversight. The 3R principle reduces environmental impact.
Key takeaways: four big metals, POPs, informal recycling risks, and bioaccumulation causing chronic health effects.
g.text: Big 4 metals: Lead. Mercury. Cadmium. Chromium. POPs: Dioxins, PCBs, PBDEs
